lines.line: > The speaker introduces himself as Stephen F Austin, a doctoral candidate at the University of Idaho, conducting an interview with Suzanne Asha Stone. The interview is part of a historical project supported by the Idaho Historical Society, focusing on the history of wolf reintroduction in Idaho. Suzanne is introduced as an expert in wildlife coexistence and wolf reintroduction efforts.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces himself as Stephen F Austin, a doctoral candidate at the University of Idaho, conducting an interview with Suzanne Asha Stone. The interview is part of a historical project supported by the Idaho Historical Society, focusing on the history of wolf reintroduction in Idaho. Suzanne is introduced as an expert in wildlife coexistence and wolf reintroduction efforts.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses the compensation program for ranchers affected by wolves and the development of non-lethal methods to promote coexistence between wolves and livestock. Suzanne highlights the successes and ongoing challenges of these programs, emphasizing the importance of adapting and improving coexistence strategies.

metadata.title: Compensation and Non-lethal Coexistence Methods
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the compensation program for ranchers affected by wolves and the development of non-lethal methods to promote coexistence between wolves and livestock. Suzanne highlights the successes and ongoing challenges of these programs, emphasizing the importance of adapting and improving coexistence strategies.

lines.line: > Suzanne shares her early experiences with wolves in Texas, which sparked her interest in wildlife and specifically wolf conservation. She recounts a pivotal moment in her youth when she encountered a red wolf, leading her to pursue a career focused on wildlife conservation and management, influenced by the writings of Barry Lopez and Aldo Leopold.

metadata.title: Early Life and Motivation
metadata.synopsis: Suzanne shares her early experiences with wolves in Texas, which sparked her interest in wildlife and specifically wolf conservation. She recounts a pivotal moment in her youth when she encountered a red wolf, leading her to pursue a career focused on wildlife conservation and management, influenced by the writings of Barry Lopez and Aldo Leopold.

lines.line: > Suzanne describes her initial involvement with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as an intern, where she began her work on wolf reintroduction. She details the early stages of reintroduction efforts, including the challenges of gathering public and political support, and the complexities of managing wolf populations in Idaho.

metadata.title: Beginning of Wolf Reintroduction Efforts
metadata.synopsis: Suzanne describes her initial involvement with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as an intern, where she began her work on wolf reintroduction. She details the early stages of reintroduction efforts, including the challenges of gathering public and political support, and the complexities of managing wolf populations in Idaho.

lines.line: # Changes in Hunting Community and Wolf Perceptions

> Suzanne reflects on the significant changes within the hunting community's perception of wolves over the years. She notes a shift from ethical hunting practices to a more aggressive stance towards wildlife, which has influenced public opinion and policy regarding wolves.

metadata.title: Changes in Hunting Community and Wolf Perceptions
metadata.synopsis: Suzanne reflects on the significant changes within the hunting community's perception of wolves over the years. She notes a shift from ethical hunting practices to a more aggressive stance towards wildlife, which has influenced public opinion and policy regarding wolves.

lines.line: > The discussion turns to how political dynamics have shaped wolf reintroduction and management efforts across different states. Suzanne points out the varying levels of success and collaboration in states like Oregon and California compared to Idaho, highlighting the impact of political will and public sentiment on wildlife conservation.

metadata.title: Political Influence on Wolf Management
metadata.synopsis: The discussion turns to how political dynamics have shaped wolf reintroduction and management efforts across different states. Suzanne points out the varying levels of success and collaboration in states like Oregon and California compared to Idaho, highlighting the impact of political will and public sentiment on wildlife conservation.

lines.line: > In the concluding remarks, Suzanne expresses concerns about the current state of wolf management in Idaho, lamenting the severe measures now being taken against wolves. She questions the ethical implications of reintroduction efforts given the current hostile environment towards wolves, suggesting a need for reevaluation of strategies and goals.

She questions the ethical implications of reintroduction efforts given the current hostile environment towards wolves, suggesting a need for reevaluation of strategies and goals.

story.title: Suzanne Asha Stone - Wolf Reintroduction May 15, 2025
story.description: The interview focuses on Suzanne Asha Stone, Executive Director of the International Wildlife Coexistence Network, discussing her role in wolf conservation and reintroduction in Idaho. Key topics include her involvement in the reintroduction efforts in the mid-1990s, the dynamics between environmental groups, governmental agencies, and local communities, and her work in compensation programs and non-lethal coexistence methods. The conversation highlights changes in public perception and hunting culture over time, the role of collaborative efforts in wildlife management, and ongoing challenges and polarization in wolf conservation. The narrative reflects on the successes and complexities of the reintroduction process and the evolving socio-political factors influencing wildlife management.
